name: Charcoal/Gas Kettle Grill (Weber-style)
heat: Convection and radiation — hot air rises and radiates from the coals/burners, plus direct conduction if food touches the grate
simple: The charcoal or gas burner creates heat that warms the air inside the closed kettle. That hot air circulates around your food like a convection oven. Meanwhile, the glowing coals or burner shoots infrared radiation upward, browning the surface. If the meat touches the metal grate, it also absorbs heat through direct contact.
expect: Temperatures reach 180-260°C. Food develops a dark, flavourful crust (the Maillard reaction) and smoke from fat dripping onto coals adds that distinct grilled flavour. Cooking takes longer than open braaiing but is more even — the kettle traps heat and moisture.
adjust: Control heat via: (1) Air vents on kettle — more air = hotter fire, (2) Coal pile depth — bank coals to one side for indirect heat, (3) Lid position — keep it closed for oven-like roasting, open for direct grilling. Use the thermometer in the lid to gauge temperature.
best_for: Large roasts (leg of lamb, chicken), ribs, pizza (with a stone), anything needing both high-heat searing and slower roasting. Great for windier days when an open fire struggles.
watch: Flare-ups when fat drips onto coals can burn food. Keep a spray bottle of water handy. Do not overuse lighter fluid — it taints flavour. Let coals ash over (grey-white) before cooking to avoid chemical taste.
sa: The kettle grill is a South African staple — every suburban home has one. It bridges the gap between the traditional braai and modern convenience. Use it for Sunday roasts or when the southeaster blows too hard for an open fire.

name: Braai (Wood & Coal Open Fire)
heat: Radiation and convection — direct infrared heat from glowing coals and flames, plus rising hot air currents
simple: Wood burns down to hot coals that radiate intense infrared heat upward, like the sun on your face. Flames leap and lick the food, adding instant char. Hot air rises from the fire, creating a natural convection current. The heat is fierce and uneven — closer to the coals means more searing, further away means slower cooking.
expect: Temperatures soar to 250-400°C directly over coals. Food develops a dark, smoky crust with a distinct wood-smoke flavour. Cooking is fast and social. The result is less even than a kettle grill — parts closer to the coals char more, but that variation is part of the charm.
adjust: Build the fire in layers: wood at the bottom, coals on top. Let it burn down to glowing embers before cooking (no angry flames, just red-hot coals). Raise or lower the grill grid to control heat intensity. Fan the coals briefly to spike temperature for searing.
best_for: Boerewors, lamb chops, steak, chicken wings, sosaties, fish wrapped in foil, grilled mielies. Anything that benefits from high heat and smoke. The braai is as much about the gathering as the food.
watch: Flare-ups from dripping fat can char food to black. Keep a clean section of grid to move food away from flames. Do not use firelighters once food is on the grid — the chemical taste ruins everything. Watch for wind shifts that blow ash onto the meat.
sa: The braai is South Africa's cultural heartbeat — more than cooking, it is a ritual of gathering, storytelling, and identity. Every family has their secret wood mix (blue gum, rooikrans, sekelbos). A true braaier knows the coals must be 'just right' — not too hot, not too cold, no flames, just a bed of glowing embers.

name: Boil & Simmer (Water on a Hob)
heat: Conduction — heat from the stove element passes through the pot into the water, which then conducts heat into the food
simple: The stove heats the metal pot, which transfers that heat to the water inside. Water circulates as it warms: hot water rises, cooler water sinks, creating a gentle mixing action. Food submerged in that water absorbs heat through direct contact. Boiling is violent bubbling at 100°C, simmering is gentle bubbles just below that.
expect: Water boils at exactly 100°C at sea level (slightly lower at high altitude like Johannesburg). Boiling cooks fast but can be rough on delicate foods. Simmering (80-95°C) cooks gently and evenly, perfect for tough cuts that need time to tenderize. No browning occurs — food stays pale and moist.
adjust: Turn the stove dial to control intensity: full blast for boiling, low medium for simmer. Watch the bubbles — steady gentle bubbles mean simmer, rolling chaotic boil means boiling. Add cold water to stop boiling instantly if needed. Use a lid to speed heating, remove it to cool down.
best_for: Pasta, potatoes, rice, boiled eggs, soups, stews, curries, poaching chicken, blanching vegetables. Simmering excels for slow-cooked dishes like oxtail, bredie, or bean soups.
watch: Pots boil over easily when cooking starches (pasta, rice, maize meal). Watch closely and stir often. Salting water raises boiling point slightly but the main effect is flavour — do not oversalt. At high altitude, water boils below 100°C so cooking takes longer.
sa: Every South African kitchen has a pot of pap on the simmer. Boiling and simmering are the backbone of our stews, bredies, and soups. Remember that in Johannesburg (1600m above sea level), water boils around 94-95°C, so adjust cooking times for dried beans and maize meal.

name: Steaming (Basket/Insert)
heat: Convection — hot steam carries heat from the boiling water into the food
simple: Boiling water produces steam, an invisible hot gas that rises and circulates around the food in the basket. Steam transfers heat when it touches the cooler food surface, condensing back into water and releasing that heat. This constant flow of hot steam cooks food gently and evenly from all sides at once.
expect: Steam cooks at 100°C (same temperature as boiling water) but gentler because food does not bang around in turbulent water. Colours stay bright, nutrients stay in the food rather than leaching into cooking water. Texture is tender but not mushy. No browning — food stays pale.
adjust: Control heat by adjusting the stove under the boiling water — more heat means more steam production. Keep the pot lid tight to trap steam. Check water level periodically and top up with boiling water from a kettle to avoid stopping the steam. Do not let the pot boil dry.
best_for: Vegetables (broccoli, carrots, butternut, spinach), fish fillets, dumplings, steamed pudding, rice (in a steamer basket), heating leftover pap without drying it out. Excellent for preserving vitamins and bright colour in greens.
watch: Steam burns are worse than boiling water burns — they penetrate deeper. Open the lid away from your face to let steam escape before removing the basket. Do not overcook — steamed vegetables go from perfect to mushy quickly. Ensure the boiling water does not touch the food.
sa: Steamed vegetables are a staple alongside braaied meat and pap. In township cooking, steamed mielie bread and steamed bread are traditional favourites. A cheap bamboo steamer basket works beautifully for fish and greens.

name: Microwave Oven
heat: Dielectric heating — microwaves agitate water molecules inside the food, creating friction heat from within
simple: The microwave oven shoots invisible electromagnetic waves (microwaves) that penetrate food. These waves make water molecules inside the food spin and vibrate millions of times per second. All that molecular rubbing creates heat, so the food warms from the inside out. The microwaves bounce around the metal oven walls to reach all parts of the food.
expect: Heating is fast and uneven — some spots get scorching hot while others stay cool. Water in food heats first and fastest, so moist areas heat more than dry spots. No browning or crisping occurs — microwaves cannot create the Maillard reaction that gives grilled food flavour. Food emerges steaming hot but pale and soft.
adjust: Power level controls heating intensity: 100% for fast heating, 50% for gentle warming. Time is the main lever — start with less and add more if needed. Stir halfway through to redistribute hot spots. Let food stand for 1-2 minutes after heating — the heat continues to equalize during this rest.
best_for: Reheating leftovers, melting butter or chocolate, steaming vegetables in a covered bowl with a splash of water, quickly heating milk for coffee, defrosting frozen food (use the defrost setting), baked potatoes in 5 minutes. Weak for: browning, crisping, searing, baking.
watch: Superheating — liquids can get dangerously hot without bubbling, then erupt when disturbed. Use a microwave-safe container and stir liquids carefully. Do not run empty — microwaves bouncing off nothing can damage the machine. Metal causes arcing and fire — no cutlery or foil inside.
sa: The microwave is essential in busy South African households for quick meals and reheating yesterday's pap or stew. In load-shedding, a generator-powered microwave keeps dinner moving. Not a substitute for a proper braai or oven, but indispensable for convenience.
